## Context

For this reason, we will call variations the differences produced by δ, and we will retain the name of differentials for those produced by d.

In order to apply this method to the case in question, we will assume that L = 0, M = 0, etc. are the equations of condition, which must exist with respect to every mass point because of the nature of the problem, and we will call them Undetermined Equations of Condition.

Conversely, there may be particular equations of condition at these points, which we will call Determined Equations of Condition to distinguish them from those which exist in general for the entire mass.

## Article 15

15. But the differentials $\mathrm{d}\delta x$ and $\mathrm{d}^2\delta y$, which appear under the symbol $\mathbf{S}$, can be eliminated by the well-known operation of integration by parts. Because, in general, $\int\Omega\mathrm{d}\delta x = \Omega\delta x - \int\delta x \mathrm{d}\Omega$, and similarly for higher orders.

Thus denoting by a prime the quantities related to the beginning of the total integrals denoted by $\mathbf{S}$ and by two primes those related to the end of these integrals, one will have the following relations:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf{S} \Omega\mathrm{d} \delta x = \Omega^{\prime\prime} \delta x^{\prime\prime} - \Omega^{\prime} \delta x^{\prime} - \mathbf{S} \delta x \mathrm{d} \Omega \\ \mathbf{S} \Omega\mathrm{d}^{2} \delta x = \Omega^{\prime\prime} \mathrm{d} \delta x^{\prime\prime} - \mathrm{d} \Omega^{\prime\prime} \delta x^{\prime\prime} + \mathbf{S} \delta x \mathrm{d}^{2} \Omega \\ \end{array}
$$

## Article 16

16. Thus in this manner the general equation of equilibrium will be reduced to the following form:

$$
\mathbf{S} (\Xi\delta x + \Sigma\delta y + \Psi\delta z) + \Lambda = 0
$$

in which $\Xi, \Sigma, \Psi$ will be functions of $x, y, z$ and their differentials.

## Article 34

34. Therefore, if the double integral $\mathbf{S}U\mathrm{d}x\mathrm{d}y$ is to be maximized or minimized, the following equation will be obtained.
